qualifying_org_definition: Churches, local government agencies (federal, state, county, city, or town), and other non-profits who are willing to supervise the individual's work and verify hours and participation. The organization must be willing to meet reporting requirements to establish participation. Unpaid work is explicitly listed as a qualifying RTW activity in SNAP Manual Section 3500 ('Unpaid work, verified under the standards established by the State agency'). Non-profit sector is explicitly included; no restriction on type of nonprofit work.
verification_instrument: DCO-261 Volunteer Agreement (Arkansas DHS form, Oct-15 revision) — completed by the supervising entity (church, local gov, or nonprofit) and used by county workers to verify volunteer hours for individuals meeting work requirements. The Comparable Workfare entity/supervisor completes the DCO-261; the eligibility worker uses it to credit RTW compliance. Described in Appendix A of SNAP Certification Manual as 'Used by counties to verify volunteer hours for individuals volunteering to meet work requirements.' (SNAP-Appendices-10.01.2025.pdf, page 6.)
hours_formula_detail: Section 3500 of AR SNAP Policy Manual explicitly states RTW requires 'work at least 20 hours per week (or an average of 80 hours a month)' for unpaid volunteer work. Note: the Comparable Workfare track uses a separate workfare formula (SNAP allotment ÷ state/federal minimum wage, whichever is greater — per Section 3730), which yields fewer hours for most recipients (e.g., ~26 hrs/mo at $291 allotment ÷ $11.00 AR minimum wage). However, the general RTW volunteer lane (Section 3500 unpaid work, DCO-261) is governed by the 80-hour federal standard, not the workfare formula.
accepts_outofstate_detail: Section 3740.2 and 3752.2 of the AR SNAP Policy Manual list Comparable Workfare providers as 'churches, local government agencies (such as any federal, state, county, city, or town), and other non-profits' with no geographic restriction on nonprofit location. The policy is silent on whether the nonprofit must be AR-based. No public source imposes an in-state requirement. Under federal default (7 CFR 273.24), any qualifying nonprofit may serve as a work site absent a state restriction; AR policy adds no such restriction. Out-of-state nonprofits should be operationally accepted, but this has not been explicitly confirmed in state policy.
geographic_waivers: none — fully enforced statewide. Section 3501 of the AR SNAP Policy Manual states: 'The state of Arkansas is currently not under a waiver and RTW applies as of January 1, 2016.' No ABAWD waiver appears on the FNA ABAWD Waivers FY2025-2029 list. OBBBA (signed July 2025) further restricts future waiver availability.
submission_deadline: unknown — the household must notify the county office when RTW is met (Section 3540.3: 'The household must notify the county office when a disqualified individual goes to work or otherwise meets RTW'); the worker must determine compliance within 10 days of reported change. For ongoing Comparable Workfare, the supervising entity maintains monthly records (Section 3752.2). No explicit calendar deadline (e.g., 'by the 10th') found in public sources.
notes:
Arkansas uses Form DCO-261 (Volunteer Agreement) as the county verification instrument for volunteer hours toward the ABAWD work requirement. This form is distinct from the E&T referral track (DCO-205) and the Workfare track (DCO-207 through DCO-212), establishing a clean general volunteer lane. No evidence of a mandatory EDP, county assignment, or provider MOU as a required pre-step before volunteer hours count. The county worker verifies hours via DCO-261 — this is the verification mechanism, not a gatekeeping hoop. The AR SNAP glossary (06/01/18) explicitly includes non-profit sector unpaid work. Arkansas administers SNAP statewide through the Division of County Operations, so policy is state-uniform (county_variation_flag=false). ABAWD enforced statewide in 2026; no waiver in effect. OBBBA age expansion (18-64) being implemented per HR1 legislative attachment. Confidence is medium because the full ABAWD policy manual chapter was not accessed — only the form list appendix (SNAP-Appendices-10.01.2025.pdf) and the SNAP glossary. The form description is unambiguous but a direct reading of the ABAWD policy chapter would raise confidence to high.

REFINEMENT PASS (2026-06-21): Conducted adversarial research to test the Tier 1 classification. Searched for mandatory E&T pre-steps, approved volunteer site lists, required EDPs, and county-level gating. Key findings: (1) GENERAL VOLUNTEER LANE CONFIRMED — multiple independent sources (AARP, Propel, federal FNA guidance) confirm Arkansas ABAWDs can satisfy the 80-hour monthly requirement through unpaid volunteer work without E&T enrollment. (2) MANDATORY E&T (April 3, 2026) — Arkansas transitioned SNAP E&T from voluntary to mandatory for beneficiaries 'not working at least 20 hours per week.' However, this mandatory E&T requirement is triggered only for those not already meeting the work threshold; under 7 CFR §273.24, an ABAWD who independently meets the 80-hour requirement through volunteer work satisfies ABAWD compliance regardless of E&T enrollment status. The mandatory E&T is a separate program layer for non-working recipients and does not gate the general volunteer lane. (3) NO PRE-APPROVED SITE LIST FOUND — no public evidence of a required pre-approved nonprofit list or MOU requirement for general (non-E&T) volunteer hours toward ABAWD. The VolunteerAR.org portal is a tool for E&T contractors and participants to find opportunities, but its use is not required for the general volunteer lane. (4) SNAP Works MIS — used by E&T providers to report hours to DHS; this is the reporting mechanism for the E&T track, not a gate on the general DCO-261 volunteer track. (5) REMAINING AMBIGUITY — The mandatory E&T trigger phrase 'not working at least 20 hours per week' is not explicitly defined in public sources to include or exclude volunteer work. If AR interprets 'working' to exclude unpaid volunteering, volunteers could theoretically face an E&T referral in addition to the DCO-261 process — but no public source confirms this interpretation and it would conflict with federal SNAP law. The AR policy manual PDFs (SNAP-Policy-Manual-04.05.2025-2-1.pdf, SNAP-Policy-Directives.pdf) were retrieved but are image-based scans and not text-searchable. Confidence remains MEDIUM: the general volunteer lane and DCO-261 track are well-established; the mandatory E&T layer (April 2026) introduces a theoretical complication that cannot be fully resolved from public sources but is unlikely to block the volunteer lane based on federal law principles.

FINAL PASS (2026-06-21): Successfully extracted text from both primary source PDFs via Wayback Machine archives (policy manual: web.archive.org/web/20250713193724; appendices: web.archive.org/web/20251030220424). Both PDFs are text-layered (not scanned images) and fully readable via pypdf. Key findings that resolve the prior ambiguity and raise confidence to HIGH: (1) SNAP Manual Section 3500 (RTW, pg 149) explicitly defines 'working in order to meet RTW' to include 'Unpaid work, verified under the standards established by the State agency' — this is the standalone general volunteer lane, independent of E&T or Workfare. (2) DCO-261 context clarified: Section 3740.2 (Comparable Workfare Referral) and Section 3751.2 show that DCO-261 is issued within the Comparable Workfare program — the eligibility worker 'Issue[s] the DCO-261 Volunteer Agreement to be completed by the Comparable Workfare entity/individual who will supervise any volunteer activity.' Comparable Workfare sites are limited to churches, local government agencies, and other nonprofits. The form confirms hours directly with the supervising entity. (3) Appendix A (forms list) confirms: DCO-261 = 'Used by counties to verify volunteer hours for individuals volunteering to meet work requirements.' No EDP, assignment, or MOU pre-step required. (4) Section 3620 E&T Referral: ABAWDs 'working twenty (20) hrs. per week (or an average of eighty (80) hours per month)' are excluded from mandatory E&T referral — this covers those meeting RTW via volunteer work. The mandatory E&T concern from the prior pass is resolved: an ABAWD meeting RTW at 80 hrs/mo via volunteer work qualifies for exemption #3 ('working 80 hours per month') and is not required to additionally enroll in E&T. (5) Section 3280 ('volunteer work is not gainful employment') applies only to the 30-hr/week general SNAP work registration exemption, not to the ABAWD RTW — the RTW statute separately and explicitly allows unpaid work. No adversarial evidence found of a pre-approved site list, MOU requirement, or mandatory EDP gating the volunteer lane. Policy is uniform statewide (state-administered through Division of County Operations). Confidence upgraded to HIGH: primary policy manual read directly; all three independent criteria (RTW definition, DCO-261 form purpose, E&T referral exemption) corroborate the Tier 1 clean classification.

EXTENDED FIELDS PASS (2026-06-21): Added five new dimension groups from direct reading of AR SNAP Policy Manual (Sections 3500, 3501, 3620, 3700, 3730, 3740.2, 3751.2, 3752.2) and Appendix A. (1) HOURS FORMULA: monthly_hours_required=80, hours_formula=federal_80 — Section 3500 explicitly states the RTW for unpaid volunteer work requires 80 hours/month (20 hrs/week). The Comparable Workfare track uses the workfare formula (allotment ÷ min wage), yielding fewer hours, but the general RTW volunteer lane uses the 80-hr federal standard. (2) OUT-OF-STATE NONPROFITS: silent — Sections 3740.2 and 3752.2 list 'other non-profits' with no geographic restriction; policy does not address out-of-state nonprofits either way. Federal default applies (no state restriction = operationally accepted). (3) GEOGRAPHIC WAIVERS: none — Section 3501 states 'Arkansas is currently not under a waiver'; confirmed by FNA FY2025-2029 waiver list (AR not listed). (4) SUBMISSION UX: paper at county office; monthly reporting cadence per Comparable Workfare monitoring requirements (Section 3752.2); no explicit calendar deadline found. (5) FORM FIELDS: DCO-261 has form fields (confirmed 'Yes' in Appendix A); actual form not publicly accessible as fillable PDF, so fields inferred from policy description — the form captures entity/supervisor identity, recipient identity (including case number per SNAP process), hours worked (likely by day or week given Workfare monitoring requirements), activity description, and period dates. Notarization not required.
